coredata.dc:description: Owing to their extremely small size, carbon-quantum-dots (CQDs) can cross biological barriers, which makes them attractive for many biomedical and other applications. CQDs can retain key-chemical features and associated functionalities of the molecular sources they are derived from, provided a suitable synthesis method is used at relative mild carbonization temperatures. Here we demonstrate that CQDs hydrothermally-derived from chitosan or 2-hydroxypropyltrimethyl ammonium-chloride (HAC)-chitosan under pressurized conditions at 180 °C have a comparable elemental and molecular composition, as determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier-transform-infrared spectroscopy. In addition, both types of CQDs generated reactive-oxygen-species as an added functionality alien to their molecular carbon sources. As a result, CQDs exhibited stronger antibacterial properties against a Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and a Gram-negative Escherichia coli strain, while both molecular HAC-chitosan as well as CQDs derived from it had stronger antibacterial properties than molecular chitosan and chitosan CQDs due to the possession of quaternary ammonium groups in HAC-chitosan. Therewith, carbonization of chitosan and HAC-chitosan yields enhanced properties that can be beneficial in a high variety of different applications, including promotion of healing and bacterial infection control, preservation of food and beverages, pesticide control in agriculture and horticulture, water treatment and in many cosmetics and personal care products.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal synthesis and characterization of chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs. (a) Structure formulas of chitosan and 2-hydroxypropyltrimethyl ammonium chloride modified chitosan (HAC-chitosan). (b) Schematics of the synthesis of chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs by hydrothermal carbonization. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs in PBS. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (indicated in the panels) in PBS.

Elemental characterization of chitosan and HAC-chitosan CQDs and their molecular carbon sources. (a) Wide-scan photo-electron binding energy spectra of chitosan and chitosan CQDs. (b) Wide-scan photo-electron binding energy spectra of HAC-chitosan and HAC-chitosan CQDs. (c) N1s narrow-scans of chitosan and chitosan CQDs. (d) N1s narrow-scans of HAC-chitosan and HAC-chitosan CQDs.

Molecular characterization of chitosan and HAC-chitosan and CQDs derived from them together with physico-chemical properties of chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs. (a) FTIR spectra of chitosan and chitosan derived CQDs. (b) FTIR spectra of HAC-chitosan and HAC-chitosan derived CQDs. (c) Diameters of chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs measured using dynamic light scattering in PBS. (d) Zeta potentials of chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs measured in demineralized water. Data represent means with error bars indicating the standard deviation over three experiments with separately prepared CQDs.

Growth curves of Gram-positive S. aureus ATC C 12600and Gram-negative E. coli ATCC 25922 in presence of chitosan and HAC-chitosan and CQDs derived from them (0.05 μg mL−1). Control data refer to growth of S. aureus in TSB and E. coli in LB in absence of CQDs. (a) S. aureus growth curves based on optical density measurements at 600 nm. (b) E. coli growth curves. Data represent means with error bars indicating the standard deviation over three experiments with separately prepared molecular solutions, CQD suspensions and differently grown bacterial cultures.

Effects of molecular chitosan and HAC-chitosan and CQDs derived from them (40 μg mL−1) on 48 h old, S. aureus ATCC 12600 biofilms. (a) Biofilm thickness after exposure to both chitosans or chitosan CQDs in TSB as a function of time. See Fig. S2 for examples of CLSM images. (b) Same as panel a, now for biomass, determined after crystal violet staining of S. aureus biofilm. (c) Same as panel b, now for Log10(CFU) cm−2. Data represent means with error bars indicating the standard deviation over three experiments with separately prepared molecular solutions, CQD suspensions and differently grown bacterial cultures. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Elemental composition and N-containing groups obtained using XPS in molecular chitosan and HAC-chitosan as well as CQDs derived from them.

Minimal Bactericidal Concentrations (MBC) of chitosan and HAC-chitosan as well as of CQDs derived from them against Gram-positive S. aureus and Gram-negative E. coli. Data represent triplicates with separately cultured bacteria with fully coinciding results in all cases.

Generation of ROS by molecular chitosan and HAC-chitosan and CQDs derived from them. All data are corrected for fluorescence intensity of DCFH dissolved in absence of CQDs and expressed in arbitrary units as means ± standard deviations over triplicate experiments with molecular chitosan and carbon quantum dots derived from chitosan and HAC-chitosan.

head.ce:title: Chemical and functional inheritance of carbon quantum dots hydrothermally-derived from chitosan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to their extremely small size, carbon-quantum-dots (CQDs) can cross biological barriers, which makes them attractive for many biomedical and other applications. CQDs can retain key-chemical features and associated functionalities of the molecular sources they are derived from, provided a suitable synthesis method is used at relative mild carbonization temperatures. Here we demonstrate that CQDs hydrothermally-derived from chitosan or 2-hydroxypropyltrimethyl ammonium-chloride (HAC)-chitosan under pressurized conditions at 180 °C have a comparable elemental and molecular composition, as determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier-transform-infrared spectroscopy. In addition, both types of CQDs generated reactive-oxygen-species as an added functionality alien to their molecular carbon sources. As a result, CQDs exhibited stronger antibacterial properties against a Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and a Gram-negative Escherichia coli strain, while both molecular HAC-chitosan as well as CQDs derived from it had stronger antibacterial properties than molecular chitosan and chitosan CQDs due to the possession of quaternary ammonium groups in HAC-chitosan. Therewith, carbonization of chitosan and HAC-chitosan yields enhanced properties that can be beneficial in a high variety of different applications, including promotion of healing and bacterial infection control, preservation of food and beverages, pesticide control in agriculture and horticulture, water treatment and in many cosmetics and personal care products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots are amongst the smallest types of nanoparticles existing [1] and possess several unique features due to their extremely small size that include, photoluminescence properties [2], good biocompatibility [3,4] and low toxicity [5]. Their small size allows for easy crossing of biological barriers, which makes carbon quantum dots attractive in high-resolution bio-imaging [6,7], biosensors [8,9], drug delivery [10–13], cancer therapy [14] and antibacterial applications [15,16]. In addition, the photoluminescence of carbon quantum dots enables them to emit light upon excitation, which can be used in bio-imaging to track the distribution of drugs within the body or to monitor biological processes in real-time.

Carbon quantum dots can be prepared by acidic oxidation, pyrolysis, laser irradiation, hydrothermal or microwave-assisted synthesis from diverse raw materials [15,17] like organic molecules including antibiotics and other antimicrobials, leaves [16], honey [18], wood [19], cigarettes [20] and even probiotic or pathogenic bacteria [21,22]. Depending on the carbonization time, temperature and pressurization applied, carbon quantum dots can retain key-chemical features and associated functionalities of their carbon sources, provided the temperatures applied remain below the degradation temperature of the source molecules [21]. Bacterially derived carbon quantum dots inherited amide groups from bacterial surface proteins, while hydrocarbon-like bacterial surface compounds were carbonized into heterocyclic aromatic-carbon structures, including graphitic nitrogen [21]. Also, antibiotics can be used as carbon sources to prepare carbon quantum dots. Carbon quantum dots derived from gentamicin sulfate [23] for instance, retained amine groups causing positive surface charge that stimulated interaction of the carbon quantum dots with bacterial cell surfaces and binding with the 30S subunit of bacterial ribosomes [24].

Hydrothermal synthesis offers precise control over both the rate of temperature increase and the final reaction temperature [25], which avoids excessive degradation of source material, a common issue with high-temperature methods like pyrolysis [26]. Moreover, it also uses water as a solvent, making it an environmentally friendly and safe approach, particularly suitable for biomedical and food- and beverage-associated applications [27,28]. As a drawback, hydrothermal synthesis requires relatively long reaction times [29]. Hydrothermally-derived carbon quantum dots prepared from citric acid and d-proline retained carboxylic acid and amine groups of d-proline [30], contributing to the chiral properties and catalytic performance of the carbon quantum dots. In the above examples, carbon quantum dots not only inherited key-chemical features from their sources, but also associated functionalities that were sometimes enhanced or complemented with additional, new ones alien to their molecular carbon sources, amongst which enhanced optical properties [31], biocompatibility, chemical stability [32], lower cytotoxicity and as a frequent, additional functionality the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). High generation of ROS has been found in carbon quantum dots carbonized from polyamine and dopamine [33,34], bis-quaternary ammonium salt [35], citric acid and l-glutathione [36], gentamicin sulfate [23] and graphite [37].

Chitosan is a naturally occurring biopolymer derived from the deacetylation of chitin [38], used in a variety of applications including promotion of healing and bacterial infection control [39], preservation of food and beverages as related to bacterial presence [40–42], pesticide control in agriculture and horticulture [43,44], water treatment as related with adsorption of heavy metals [45,46] and in many cosmetics and personal care products [47,48] (skin- and hair-care, antibacterial oral health care products). Several of the applications listed above are based on the antibacterial functionality of chitosan and would benefit from enhanced antibacterial functionality of chitosan-derived carbon quantum dots, as potentially offered by ROS generating chitosan-derived carbon quantum dots.

This research focuses on deriving carbon quantum dots from chitosan using hydrothermal synthesis with the aim of retaining the chitosan key-chemistry and associated functionalities, while at the same time adding the new, additional functionality of ROS generation [49], hypothesizing that herewith the known antibacterial functionality of chitosan [50] will be enhanced. Hydrothermal synthesis was employed for carbonization, as it yields precise temperature control [25] and prevents excessive degradation of the source molecules when carried out at 180 °C [21]. The work will be based on naturally occurring chitosan and more antibacterially active, 2-hydroxypropyltrimethyl ammonium chloride modified chitosan (HAC-chitosan) [51,52].

Chitosan (deacetylation ≥ 95 %), 2-hydroxypropyltrimethyl ammonium chloride chitosan (HAC-chitosan) and acetic acid (99.5 %) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). Crystal violet solution and 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) were purchased was from Sigma-Aldrich (China). Dialysis membranes with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1 kDa were purchased from Biosharp, Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Ethanol and acetone were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China).

For the synthesis of chitosan carbon quantum dots (chitosan CQDs), 2 g of chitosan was dissolved in 2 % acetic acid solution (18 mL) and the mixed solution was added in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (Shanghai HongGuan Instrument & Equipment Co., Ltd, China). For synthesis of HAC-chitosan carbon quantum dots (HAC-chitosan CQDs), 2 g of HAC-chitosan was dissolved in 18 mL demineralized water and added to a Teflon-lined autoclave. Subsequently, Teflon-lined autoclaves were placed in an oven and subjected to pressurized, hydrothermal treatment. Temperature was increased at a rate of 5 °C per minute till 180 °C and kept at 180 °C for 12 h. These conditions were previously found to be optimal for retaining key-chemistries of probiotic and pathogenic bacteria [21]. After 12 h, the autoclave was cooled down at ambient temperature. The resulting solution, exhibiting a dark brown color, was centrifuged at 12,000 g for 20 min and the supernatant containing brown-colored carbon quantum nanoparticles was filtered through a 0.22 μm polyvinylidene difluoride membrane filter, followed by dialysis against demineralized water (cut-off 1 kDa) for 48 h under stirring at room temperature and the dialyzed solution was freeze dried to obtain quantum carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physico-chemical characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: For UV − vis absorption spectroscopy, chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs were suspended in phosphate buffered saline (PBS; 10 mM potassium phosphate and 150 mM NaCl) at pH 7.4 in a quartz cuvette. Absorption spectra were taken over the wavelength range from 200 nm to 600 nm using a microplate reader (Thermo, Varioskan Flash). The same CQDs suspensions were used to measure fluorescence emission spectra using a Jasco FP-8300 (Japan) spectrofluorometer at different excitation wavelengths between 340 nm and 420 nm.

Quantum yields were subsequently calculated versus a quinine sulfate solution as a standard (0.1 M H2SO4; quantum yield 54 % [53]), according to
(1)
Φ x

Φ

in which Φx and Φst are the quantum yields of the CQDs to be determined and the standard applied, respectively, Kx and Kst are the slopes of a linear regression of the absorbance measured at different wavelengths as a function of the integrated photoluminescence calculated for each of the different wavelengths applied. ηx and ηst are the refractive indices of the nanoparticle suspension and the standard solution, respectively.

The elemental surface composition of CQDs, freeze-dried according to published protocols [54] were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). To this end, chitosan and HAC-chitosan were pressed in small stainless-steel cups, while CQDs were pressed on gold-coated glass slides and put in the XPS chamber. XPS measurements were carried out according to previously published procedures [21]. Briefly, the chamber was brought to a vacuum of 10-9 Pa. X-rays (10 kV, 22 mA) were produced using an aluminum anode and had a spot size of 250 × 1,000 μm. Wide-scans were taken over the binding energy range of 1–1200 eV at low resolution. The area under each peak in wide-scan spectra, after background subtraction, was used for the calculation of peak intensities, yielding elemental surface concentrations. High resolution, narrow-scans were recorded of the N1s photoelectron binding energy peaks over a 20 eV binding energy range. N1s narrow-scans were decomposed into different, Gaussian peak components due to nitrogen bound to different atoms, employing full-widths-at-half-maximum of 1.4 eV and 1.6 eV for different N1s peak components, using the Casa XPS software package (Casa Software Ltd, Teignmouth, UK).

FTIR spectra (FTIR, Nicolet-20DXB, US) were taken from freeze-dried carbon quantum dots or their molecular sources by mixing with KBr (1:125) and pressing into a KBr-tablet. FTIR spectra were recorded over the wavenumber range of 4000 to 500 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. A total of 32 scans were taken and averaged.

Diameters and zeta potentials of the chitosan and HAC-chitosan CQDs were measured on carbon quantum dots suspended in demineralized water, using dynamic light scattering (Malvern ZetaSizer ZS2000, UK).

ROS generation by the carbon quantum dots and their molecular sources was assessed through the oxidation of the non-fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCFH) to the highly fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). Initially, a DCFH solution was prepared by combining 10 mg mL−1 of DCFH-DA dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide for 30 min in the presence of 0.01 M NaOH, under dark conditions and at room temperature. Subsequently, the mixture was neutralized using PBS at pH 7.4, resulting in a final concentration of 10 μg mL−1 of DCFH. Freshly prepared molecular chitosan and HAC-chitosan and carbon quantum dot suspensions (200 μL, 40 μg mL−1) in PBS were added to 96-well plates, followed by the addition of 10 μL of DCFH and incubation in the dark (4 h at 37 °C). Fluorescence intensity was measured using a spectrophotometer (Synergy H1, BioTek, USA) with excitation and emission wavelengths set at 500 nm and 535 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of DCF dissolved in PBS in absence of CQDs was found to be 4.6 ± 0.5 (a.u.) and subtracted from the fluorescence intensities measured in presence of CQDs reported.

Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 12600 and Gram-negative Escherichia coli ATCC 25922 were grown from frozen stocks (7 % DMSO, kept at −80 °C) on blood agar plates at 37 °C for 24 h. For pre-cultures, a single bacterial colony was transferred into 10 mL Tryptone Soy Broth (TSB, OXOID, Basingstoke, UK) or Lysogeny Broth (LB, OXOID, Basingstoke, UK) and incubated 24 h at 37 °C. For main cultures, the pre-culture was transferred into 200 mL TSB or LB and incubated for 16 h at 37 °C. Then, bacteria were harvested by centrifugation (5000 g, 5 min, 10 °C) followed by washing twice in sterile PBS. Finally, the bacterial suspension was sonicated (Vibra cell model 375, Sonics and Material Inc., Danbury, CT, USA) 3 times for 10 s with 30 s intervals between each cycle on ice to obtain a suspension with single bacteria. The bacterial concentrations in the suspensions were determined in a Bürker-Türk counting chamber.

Growth curves of S. aureus ATCC 12600 in TSB and E. coli ATCC 25922 in LB with added molecular chitosan and HAC-chitosan or quantum carbon dots derived from them (0.05 μg mL−1), were obtained by monitoring the optical density of the cultures at 600 nm during 20 h incubation at 37 °C. Growth curves of bacteria in growth medium without additions were used as controls.

To determine the MBCs of the two chitosans and the carbon quantum dots derived from them against the Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial strain, 100 μL of a chitosan or HAC-chitosan CQD suspension (16 μg mL−1) or 100 μL of a chitosan or HAC-chitosan solution (256 μg mL−1) in PBS were put in a 96 wells plate and 100 μL of growth medium (TSB for S. aureus ATCC 12600 and LB for E. coli ATCC 25922) was added. Then, solutions were mixed and two-fold serially diluted. Next, 10 μL bacterial suspension (1 × 105 bacteria mL−1) was added to the mixed solutions in the 96 wells plates. After 24 h incubation at 37 °C, 10 μL aliquots were taken out of a well and placed on agar plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. MBC was taken as the lowest concentration at which no visible colonies were observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biofilm exposure to carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: For biofilm experiments, S. aureus suspensions (1 × 109 bacteria mL−1, 200 μL per well) were put into sterile polystyrene 96-well plates for 2 h at room temperature to allow bacterial sedimentation and adhesion. Next, suspensions were removed, and the wells were washed 3 times with sterile PBS, filled with fresh TSB and incubated for 48 h at 37 °C for biofilm formation under static conditions. Growth medium was replaced with fresh medium after 24 h. Resulting 48 h biofilms were washed three times with sterile PBS and exposed to 200 μL of fresh medium supplemented with molecular chitosan or HAC-chitosan or CQDs derived from them at 40 μg mL−1. After exposure at 37 °C under static conditions for different periods of time up to 72 h, non-adherent bacteria were removed by washing once with PBS. Supplemented growth medium was replaced every 24 h. Subsequently, 175 μL of a 2.3 % (w/v) crystal violet solution was added to each well for biomass determination. After 30 min at room temperature, excess crystal violet solution was removed by washing four times with 200 μL of ultrapure water. Finally, the crystal violet bound to the biofilm was released by adding 225 μL of ethanol/acetone (80/20 (v/v)) to each well and the OD575 nm was measured to evaluate the biofilm mass.

For confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Leica TCS SP2 Leica, Germany), biofilms grown as described above, were stained with a cell wall damage stain [55,56] (BacLightTM, Molecular probes, The Netherlands) containing SYTO9 and propidium iodide for 20 min in the dark. After 20 min, the stain was removed and biofilms were immersed in PBS and imaged using CLSM. An argon ion laser at 488 nm to excite SYTO9 (green fluorescent) and a HeNe laser at 543 nm to excite propidium iodide (red fluorescent) were used, collecting fluorescence at 500 − 535 nm (SYTO9) and 583 − 688 nm (propidium iodide). SYTO9 is staining bacteria with an intact cell wall, while propidium iodide is staining cell wall damaged bacteria. Biofilm images were analyzed using ImageJ and to determine biofilm thickness.

For Colony Forming Unit (CFU) enumeration, after exposure to PBS as a control, molecular chitosan or HAC-chitosan or CQDs derived from them, biofilms were removed from the well by scraping and suspended in PBS. The resulting bacterial suspension was diluted and plated on the TSB agar for CFU enumeration after 24 h incubation at 37 °C.
All comparisons of MBCs and effects of carbon quantum dots on biofilms were performed with a two-tailed Student t-test, accepting significance at p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Structure formulas of chitosan and 2-hydroxypropyltrimethyl ammonium chloride modified chitosan (HAC-chitosan) are shown in Fig. 1
a, clearly indicating the difference between chitosan and HAC-chitosan by the possession of a hydrocarbon side chain with –OH and quaternary ammonium groups in HAC-chitosan. The carbonization procedure for deriving carbon quantum dots is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 b. Both chitosan CQDs as well as HAC-chitosan CQDs display strong UV–vis absorbance centered around 286 nm and 226 nm for chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs, respectively (Fig. 1 c), characteristic of carbon quantum dots. Fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 1 d) were combined with absorption spectra (Fig. 1 d) for calculation of the quantum yields of chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs ( Fig. S1 ). Relative to quinine sulfate as a standard, quantum yields of chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs were found to be 29 % and 21 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical inheritance of carbon quantum dots from their carbon sources
ce:section.ce:para:
XPS was applied to evaluate the inheritance of key-chemical elemental features of chitosan (Fig. 2
a) and HAC-chitosan (Fig. 2 b) by their respective carbon quantum dots. Molecular chitosan possesses a high at% of carbon and oxygen, with 7.0 at% nitrogen, solely comprised in –NH2 groups (Fig. 2 c; N1s peak component at 399.3 eV). The oxygen over nitrogen ratio is slightly higher than 4 (4.4; see Table 1 ), in line with the molecular structure of chitosan (see Fig. 1 a). Molecular HAC-chitosan uniquely contains chlorine and possesses less nitrogen in –NH2 groups compared with chitosan, but here nitrogen is comprised in −N+(CH3)3 groups (Fig. 2 d; N1s peak component at 403.4 eV). Note that not all –NH2 groups were substituted by −N+(CH3)3 in HAC-chitosan molecular structure. The oxygen over nitrogen ratio in HAC-chitosan (3.1) is slightly lower than in chitosan due to the presence of quaternary ammonium groups (see Table 1), in line with the molecular structure of HAC-chitosan (see also Fig. 1 a). XPS analyses demonstrate a loss of oxygen upon carbonization of both chitosan and HAC-chitosan (Fig. 2 a and 2b, respectively and Table 1). Note chlorine in HAC-chitosan is preserved upon carbonization. Concurrent with a loss of oxygen upon carbonization, the nitrogen content of CQDs obtained from HAC-chitosan increased by 30 %. N1s narrow-scans as shown in Fig. 2 c and 2d show that both chitosan and HAC-chitosan generate pyrrolic nitrogen (N-C2H) structures upon carbonization with an N1s peak component at 400.4 eV, which is crucial for producing ROS.

FTIR spectroscopy was applied to evaluate the inheritance of key-chemical molecular features of chitosan and HAC-chitosan by their respective carbon quantum dots. Chitosan displayed distinctive absorption bands at 3448 cm−1 due to OH and NH stretching vibrations of amine groups, at 2926 and 2850 cm−1 due to CH stretching vibrations, at 1643 and 1592 cm−1 due to NH bending vibrations and at 1130–1064 cm−1 due to CH bending vibrations of the pyranose ring (Fig. 3
a). Spectra of CQDs demonstrated a decrease in the absorption bands due to OH and NH stretching vibrations at 3438 cm−1 and a full loss of CH vibrations at 1130–1064 cm−1. Alternatively, the absorption at 1632 cm−1 due to amino groups increased as compared with source chitosan. These changes suggest degradation of the chitosan chain and decomposition of the pyranose ring, while functional –NH2 groups were retained and actually created upon carbonization. The FTIR spectrum of HAC-chitosan (Fig. 3 b) distinguishes itself from the spectrum of chitosan by the possession of a broader and stronger OH/NH stretching band around 3500 cm−1 due to extra –OH and –NH2 groups in the hydrocarbon chain of HAC-chitosan, a stronger CH stretching band around 2800 cm−1 due to extra CH bonds on the hydrocarbon side chains and band around 1488 cm−1 due to − N+(CH3)3 in the quaternary ammonium groups of HAC-chitosan (compare Fig. 1). HAC-chitosan CQDs lost and inherited the same key-chemical features from its source HAC-chitosan as did chitosan CQDs from its chitosan source, but in addition also inherited − N+(CH3)3 groups with an absorption band at 1488 cm−1. Collectively, the combined XPS and FTIR data show that CQDs derived from chitosan and HAC-chitosan inherit the key-chemical features from their carbon sources.

Chitosan and HAC-chitosan CQDs have a statistically similar average diameter of 5.4 ± 0.9 nm and 6.6 ± 1.1 nm (Fig. 3 c). The zeta potential (Fig. 3 d) of HAC-chitosan CQDs (+36.4 ± 1.2 mV) is significantly more positive than that of chitosan CQDs (+27.7 ± 2.6 mV) due to the presence of quaternary amino groups (−N+(CH3)3 in addition to the –NH2 groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional inheritance of carbon quantum dots from their carbon sources: antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: Antibacterial activity can be assessed in different metrics. Measurement of bacterial growth as a function of time provides a kinetic metric. Gram-positive S. aureus and Gram-negative E. coli in presence of low concentrations of carbon quantum dots derived from chitosan as well as from HAC-chitosan demonstrated significantly slower or near absence of growth (HAC-chitosan CQDs) in comparison with their molecular carbon sources (Fig. 4 ).

MBC provides a static metric of antibacterial activity that goes beyond slowing down of growth and extends to bacterial killing. Accordingly, MBC values are much higher than the concentrations of chitosan and HAC-chitosan CQDs yielding slower growth. In line with the growth kinetics provided in Fig. 4, CQDs derived from chitosan as well as from HAC-chitosan had significantly lower MBC values than their molecular carbon sources with respect to Gram-positive S. aureus and Gram-negative E. coli (Table 2 ).

In order to determine whether these advantages of chitosan in carbon quantum dots over molecular chitosan extended to bacterial biofilms, 48 h old S. aureus biofilms were exposed for different times up to 72 h to solutions of both chitosans or CQDs derived from these sources. Since it is known that MBC values against bacterial biofilms can easily be several hundreds of times higher than against planktonic bacteria [57] as involved in MBC determination, anti-biofilm activity was evaluated at a concentration of 40 µg mL−1. Neither exposure to molecular chitosan nor HAC-chitosan solutions were able to cause major reductions in biofilm thickness (Fig. 5
a), biomass (Fig. 5 b) and CFU cm−2 (Fig. 5 c). However, after carbonization, chitosan and HAC-chitosan CQDs both displayed significant reductions in biofilm thickness, biomass and CFU cm−2. Reductions were significantly larger for CQDs derived from HAC-chitosan than for CQDs derived from chitosan. Note from the CLSM images underlying Fig. 5 a and b (see Fig. S2 ), that the percentage of cell wall damaged bacteria in remaining biofilm after exposure to chitosan was smallest (28 %), followed by HAC-chitosan (36 %), but increased considerably upon exposure to the respective CQDs to 47 % and 89 % for chitosan CQDs and HAC-chitosan CQDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS generation as an additional functionality of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: ROS generation by molecular chitosan and HAC-chitosan was virtually zero, but their CQDs, most notably those derived from HAC-chitosan generated significant amounts of ROS a new, additional functionality (Table 3 ).

In this article, we demonstrate that carbon quantum dots hydrothermally derived from chitosan or HAC-chitosan have an elemental and molecular composition comparable with their source molecules. Both types of CQDs generated ROS as an added functionality alien to their molecular sources and exhibited stronger antibacterial properties than their molecular sources due to ROS generation. This confirms our hypothesis that the known antibacterial functionality of chitosans [58] can be enhanced by carbonization. Possession of HAC in the inheritance of CQDs derived from HAC-chitosan contributed to stronger antibacterial properties as compared with chitosan or chitosan CQDs.

The carbon quantum dots synthesized here were hydrothermally carbonized under pressurized conditions at 180 °C. Previous work on carbon quantum dots derived from probiotic bacteria [21,59] has demonstrated that these carbonization conditions are crucial for retaining specific key-chemistries, in the case at hand, chitosan and HAC. Thermal degradation in water in absence of pressurization of chitosan, carbon dioxide, ammonia, acetone and acetic acid generally starts at temperatures around 200 °C to 300 °C [60]. Thus, during our carbonization applied, amine side groups (–NH2) remain intact, as demonstrated here using XPS (Fig. 2 and Table 1) and FTIR (Fig. 3 a and b). This implies that carbonization of chitosan under the conditions applied does not occur through extensive degradation. Instead, chitosan is subject to high pressure induced transformation including removal of water molecules, known to occur at 180 °C under pressurized conditions, and partial decomposition of the polymer chain leading to the nucleation and growth of CQDs [61]. During this partial decomposition of the chitosan polymer chain, the glycosidic linkage and the pyranose ring within chitosan are disrupted, while nitrogen-containing groups, mainly –NH2 containing groups, will form pyrrolic nitrogen. Also, HAC-chitosan will be subject to high pressure induced transformation, with the chitosan component degrading as described above for chitosan. The HAC component is in essence a quaternary ammonium that starts to degrade at temperatures above 250 °C [62], well above the carbonization temperature of 180 °C applied. This implies that under the current conditions, quaternary ammonium groups in HAC-chitosan CQDs will be fully retained, as evidenced by our XPS (Fig. 2 and Table 1) and FTIR (Fig. 3 a and b) analyses and confirmed by positive zeta potentials (Fig. 3 d).

Positive zeta potentials of HAC-chitosan and CQDs derived from it, stimulate interaction with negatively charged bacterial cell surfaces [63,64], which is important in order to take profit out of the added functionality of ROS generation by the CQDs. ROS is generally short-lived (less than 10−5 s) [65,66] and therefore must be generated close to its target side in order to interact with it and cause cell wall damage and bacterial death before the reactive-oxygen species recombine and become inactive. The pyrrolic nitrogen formed upon carbonization, may play a major role in the generation of ROS by the CQDs synthesized, as it can facilitate the reduction of oxygen [67]. Pyrrolic nitrogen is integrated in the CQD structure [68] and possesses electron-rich sites, i.e. the lone pair of electrons on the pyrrolic nitrogen atoms facilitating catalytic electron transfer to oxygen molecules [69] from which superoxide anions and other ROS are formed, leading to a high ROS production [70]. In addition, the quaternary ammonium groups (−N+(CH3)3) present in HAC-chitosan CQDs provide a positive charge that increases their electrostatic double-layer attraction to negatively charged bacterial cell surfaces [63,64]. This attraction promotes closer contact between the CQDs and bacterial membranes, leading to more localized ROS production. The short-lived nature of ROS [65,66] requires their generation in close proximity to bacterial cells for maximum effectiveness. The combination of pyrrolic nitrogen facilitating ROS production and the positive charge enhancing bacterial interaction explains the higher antibacterial activity of HAC-chitosan derived CQDs compared to chitosan-derived CQDs.

This work is not geared towards synthesizing new antimicrobials with higher efficacy than currently used antimicrobials possess, but at understanding the carbonization conditions that lead to maintenance of the key-chemistry and added functionalities of CQDs as compared with their molecular sources. Nevertheless, in a practical sense, the enhanced antibacterial activities of chitosan and HAC-chitosan CQDs viz a viz their molecular sources imply that formulations incorporating CQDs can be more efficient, requiring less material to achieve the same or even enhanced antibacterial effects. For instance, in applications such as water purification or food packaging, where high antimicrobial performance is crucial, lower concentrations of CQDs could result in cost savings, less material usage, and fewer potential side effects. Additionally, because CQDs can penetrate biofilms more effectively, as demonstrated in this article, they provide enhanced efficacy in environments where bacteria are more resistant to treatment, such as when growing in a biofilm-mode on surfaces.

In general, the small size of carbon quantum dots and their ability to cross biological barriers make them attractive in many different applications [6–16], but at the same time necessitates thorough evaluation of their safety risks in environmental, several industrial and biomedical applications. However, safety concerns of potential application of chitosan or HAC-chitosan derived CQDs are probably minimal. First of all, carbon quantum dots derived from natural sources, such as chitosan or other biopolymers [73], are known for their favourable biodegradability and low toxicity, making them highly suitable for environmental, industrial and biomedical applications. Secondly, carbon quantum dots synthesized from natural sources like chitosan, break down into non-toxic by-products under environmental conditions, thereby reducing their long-term impact [74]. In biomedical applications, their small size reduces the chances of immune cell uptake [75] and removal from the body usually occurs through the renal system [76]. Accordingly, it has been consistently demonstrated that carbon quantum dots do not cause significant harm to mammalian cells or aquatic organisms [77]. Collectively, carbon quantum dots derived from chitosan offer a promising, sustainable alternative to molecular chitosan, with minimal risk and relatively high safety in environmental, industrial and biomedical applications.

The present study demonstrates the successful synthesis of carbon quantum dots from chitosan and HAC-chitosan via hydrothermal synthesis, retaining key-chemical features from their molecular sources while introducing the novel, added functionality of ROS generation. These CQDs exhibited significantly enhanced antibacterial activities, compared to their non-carbonized molecular forms, largely attributable to ROS generation, which was absent in non-carbonized molecular forms. The results support the notion that carbonization of chitosan enhances its antibacterial functionality by ROS generation, causing bacterial cell wall damage and killing. This work represents a distinct advancement over previous studies on CQDs, most of which focus on optical or biomedical function [7,78]. Several studies on antibacterial application of CQDs [79,80] lost sight of the role of ROS in enhancing antibacterial activity. The findings establish, for the first time, that CQDs derived from chitosan display good antibacterial activity due to ROS production, contributing significantly to the field by introducing a new mechanism for improving antibacterial performance of an antimicrobial. Future research may benefit from investigating the interaction of these CQDs with various bacterial strains in more complex biological systems to more fully explore their potential as non-antibiotic antibacterial agents as well as their performance and biosafety in specific applications such as preservation of food and beverages, pesticide control in agriculture and horticulture, water treatment and in cosmetics and personal care products. Further exploration of the structural properties of CQDs, aimed at optimizing ROS generation, could enhance their antibacterial effectiveness.
